book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

classification.cautionReason: Conciliar or creedal material received in mainstream Nicene Christian tradition, with denominational differences on some canons.

The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.

metadata.author: The Fourth Ecumenical Council. The Council of Chalcedon

title: Canon XXVI.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Forasmuch as we have heard that in certain churches the bishops managed the church-business without stewards, it has seemed good that every church having a bishop shall have also a steward from among its own clergy, who shall manage the church business under the sanction of his own bishop; that so the administration of the church may not be without a witness; and that thus the goods of the church may not be squandered, nor reproach be brought upon the priesthood; and if he [i.e., the Bishop] will not do this, he shall be subjected to the divine canons.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Ancient Epitome of Canon XXVI.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The Œconomus in all churches must be chosen from the clergy.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: And the bishop who neglects to do this is not without blame.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: As the stream of offerings became fuller, the work of dispensing them became more complex, until the archdeacons could no longer find time for it, and it was committed to a special officer called “œconomus” or steward (Bingham, iii, 12, 1; Transl. of Fleury, iii., 120).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: So the Council of Gangra, in the middle of the fourth century, forbids the church offerings to be disposed of without consent of the bishop or of the person appointed, εἰς οἰκονομίαν εὐποιϊασ (canon viij.); and St. Basil mentions the œconomi of his own church (Epist., xxiij.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: 1), and the “ταμίαι of the sacred goods” of his brother’s at Nyssa (ib., 225).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: And although Gregory Nazianzen took credit to himself for declining to appoint a “stranger” to make an estimate of the property which of right belonged to the church of Constantinople, and in fact, with a strange confusion between personal and official obligations, gave the go-by to the whole question (Carm. de Vita sua, 1479 ff.), his successor, Nectarius, being a man of business, took care to appoint a “church-steward”; and Chrysostom, on coming to the see, examined his accounts, and found much superfluous expenditure (Palladius, Dial, p.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Theophilus of Alexandria compelled two of the Tall Brothers to undertake the οἰκονομία of the Alexandrian church (Soc., vi.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: 7); and in one of his extant directions observes that the clergy of Lyco wish for another “œconomus,” and that the bishop has consented, in order that the church-funds may be properly spent (Mansi, iii., 1257).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: At Hippo St. Augustine had a “præpositus domus” who acted as Church-steward (Possidius, Vit.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: August., xxiv.).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Isidore of Pelusium denounces Martinianus as a fraudulent “œconomus,” and requests Cyril to appoint an upright one (Epist. ii., 127), and in another letter urges him to put a stop to the dishonest greed of those who acted as stewards of the same church (ib., v.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: The records of the Council of Ephesus mention the “œconomus” of Constantinople, the “œconomus” of Ephesus (Mansi, iv., 1228–1398), and, the “œconomus” of Philadelphia.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: According to an extant letter of Cyril, the “œconomi” of Perrha in Syria were mistrusted by the clergy, who wished to get rid of them “and appoint others by their own authority” (ib., vii., 321).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Ibas of Edessa had been complained of for his administration of church property; he was accused, e.g., of secreting a jewelled chalice, and bestowing the church revenues, and gold and silver crosses, on his brother and cousins; he ultimately undertook to appoint “œconomi” after the model of Antioch (Mansi, vii., 201).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Proterius, afterwards patriarch of Alexandria and a martyr for Chalcedonian orthodoxy, was “œconomus” under Dioscorus (ib., iv., 1017), as was John Talaia, a man accused of bribery, under his successor (Evag., iii., 12).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: There may have been many cases in which there was no “œconomus,” or in which the management was in the hands of private agents of the bishop, in whom the Church could put no confidence; and the Council, having alluded to the office of “œconomus” in canons ij. and xxv., now observes that some bishops had been managing their church property without “œconomi,” and thereupon resolves “that every church which has a bishop shall also have an œconomus” from among its own clergy, to administer the property of the church under the direction of its own bishop; so that the administration of the church property may not be unattested, and thereby waste ensue, and the episcopate incur reproach.”

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Any bishop who should neglect to appoint such an officer should be punishable under “the divine” (or sacred) “canons.”

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: Nearly three years after the Council, Leo saw reason for requesting Marcian not to allow civil judges, “novo exemplo,” to audit the accounts of “the œconomi of the church of Constantinople,” which ought, “secundum traditum morem,” to be examined by the bishop alone (Epist. cxxxvij.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: In after days the “great steward” of St. Sophia was always a deacon; he was a conspicuous figure at the Patriarch’s celebrations, standing on the right of the altar, vested in alb and stole, and holding the sacred fan (ῥιπίδιον); his duty was to enter all incomings and outgoings of the church’s revenue in a charterlary, and exhibit it quarterly, or half yearly, to the patriarchs; and he governed the church during a vacancy of the see (Eucholog., pp.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: In the West, Isidore of Seville describes the duties of the “œconomus”; he has to see to the repair and building of churches, the care of church lands, the cultivation of vineyards, the payment of clerical stipends, of doles to the widows and the poor, and of food and clothing to church servants, and even the carrying on of church law suits,—all “cum jussu et arbitrio sui episcopi” (Ep. to Leudefred, Op. ii., 520); and before Isidore’s death the IVth Council of Toledo refers to this canon, and orders the bishops to appoint “from their own clergy those whom the Greeks call œconomi, hoc est, qui vici episcoporum res ecclesiasticas tractant (canon xlviij., Mansi, x, 631).

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: There was an officer named “œconomus” in the old Irish monasteries; see Reeves’ edition of Adamnan, p.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: This Canon is found twice in the Corpus Juris Canonici, Gratian’s Decretum, Pars II., Causa XVI., Q.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: VII, Canon xxi., and again in Pars I., Dist.

verses.book: The XXX Canons of the Holy and Fourth Synods, of Chalcedon.
verses.text: LXXXIX., c. iv.
